The alert came through Iris's relay at eleven forty-three at night, and Corin was awake because he had been reviewing the content analysis preliminary data that the technical analyst had sent at nine — the forty-seven unrouted records had substrate content worth reading, and he had been reading it.

He read the alert.

The Reckoner network's alert format was standardized: a brief header with the alert category, a coordinate reference in the northern zone's mapping grid, a time stamp for when the monitoring system had first detected the signal disruption, and a status summary in three lines. The header read: *ACTIVE DISRUPTION — NODE CLUSTER — NORTHERN SUPPLY ROUTE*. The coordinate reference placed the cluster at a maintenance access point he had driven past twice during his time in Dreya Cole's settlement, a spot on the road where the maintenance track branched off into the supply route's eastern service corridor. The time stamp was eleven twenty-two — twenty-one minutes before the relay reached him. The status summary: *Three nodes, cluster configuration. Signal disruption consistent with portable blocking equipment. Unrestored territory adjacency: none — supply corridor, active settlement use.*

Six remaining Congregation fighters. A node cluster on the northern supply route. Monitoring range — the Reckoner network covered the northern transitional zone. Corin was in the northern transitional zone. He was staying at the settlement house in Dreya Cole's settlement, where the reconciliation framework session with the eight new participants had concluded three hours ago. He was forty minutes from the node cluster.

He sat with the alert for thirty seconds. He called Hollan.

"I've seen it," Hollan said. "Security team is fifteen minutes behind you. Don't go."

"I'm the closest Archive presence with framework authority. If I'm there before the security team, there's a conversation to be had."

"If you're there before the security team, you're between active fighters and a node cluster with no security backup."

"I know. I'm going."

"Corin —"

"Tell the security team I'm forty minutes ahead of them. Tell them to come in without escalation posture."

He ended the call. He put on his coat. He went to the door of the settlement house and hesitated for exactly one second, then walked out.

Dreya Cole was in the settlement house's common area — she had been there since the session ended, reviewing the session documentation with two of her settlement council members. She looked at him when he came through.

"The northern node cluster," she said. It was not a question. The alert had gone through the Timelost network too.

"Six of the remaining fighters. I'm going."

She stood. "I'm calling the security team from your end. My relay is faster."

"Hollan's team is already moving."

"My relay is faster." She was already at the communication panel. "Go."

He drove fast on the northern road, which was maintained because it was a supply route and because the Congregation had struck it before and the settlements in the area had organized to keep it passable. The road was graded gravel for the first twelve kilometers and then narrowed to compacted earth where the supply route left the settled zone and entered the northern transitional corridor — the border between the eastern communities' territory and the unrestored landmass to the north. The vehicle's navigation system showed the maintenance track turnoff at thirty-seven minutes out. He made it in thirty-three.

The monitoring alert updated twice during the drive. The first update, fourteen minutes in: signal disruption active, three nodes blocked, no escalation detected in the network status. The second update, at twenty-six minutes: three fighters had withdrawn from the node cluster, withdrawing northeast along the maintenance track. Three remained in contact with the cluster. Security team status at the second update: twenty-two minutes behind him.

He arrived at the node cluster at twelve twenty-three in the morning.

The cluster was three monitoring nodes mounted on a post assembly at the edge of the supply route's maintenance track. The post assembly was a standard northern zone installation — the kind built for supply corridors where the substrate variance ran high enough to require active stabilization infrastructure. The posts were three meters tall, galvanized, with the node housings mounted at the top and the emitter arrays at the base where the foundation anchor plates sat in the compacted ground. The spacing between the three posts was twelve meters, wide enough that the combined emitter coverage overlapped in the center of the supply lane. One of the three nodes was damaged — the housing was cracked along its upper seam and the indicator lights were dark. The other two were intact, their emitter arrays still running, their indicator lights cycling through the standard operational pattern: green-green-amber, a three-second interval. Around the post assembly, a security zone had been established by the three remaining fighters using the kind of equipment the Congregation had been using for the last two years — portable signal blockers, positional holds, the hardware of a movement that had been operational long enough to develop consistent tactics.

The three fighters were standing inside the signal zone. One of them was a man Corin recognized from the reconciliation framework documentation, though he had not met him directly: Fen Corr, thirty-eight years old, who had left the Congregation before Joren's walk to the Citadel and had returned when the six-fighter operational unit reformed after the acknowledgment motion passed. He had left and come back. The reconciliation framework's documentation noted his departure as a detail without characterization.

The Archive security contingent — four people, standard operational equipment, no escalation posture as Corin had requested — arrived eighteen minutes after him and held position thirty meters out. Corin was standing between the fighters and the security contingent, which he had positioned himself at deliberately and which meant he was not, technically, a neutral party. He was not neutral. He was the Restoration Liaison.

"The acknowledgment motion passed two days ago," he said, to Fen Corr specifically, because Fen Corr was the one looking at him. "The eastern communities' formal engagement is active. The eight fighters who contacted us through Ash are in the reconciliation framework — they were in the session tonight, three hours ago. What does this strike achieve that those things don't?"

The two other fighters were watching the security contingent. Fen Corr was watching Corin.

"The node is still extracting," he said.

Corin looked at the node cluster. The damaged node. The two intact ones.

"These are stabilization nodes," he said. "They're part of the northern supply route's Resonance stabilization infrastructure. They prevent substrate fluctuation in the supply corridor so the settlements can run regular supply transport without signal interference."

"Show me the difference," Fen Corr said.

Corin looked at him. The man's voice was not aggressive. It was the voice of someone asking a genuine question — not a tactical question, not a delaying question. A genuine one. He had said *show me the difference* the way someone says it when they have been told a thing is true and have not been shown the evidence.

Corin walked to the intact node — the closest one, a unit two meters tall, the monitoring housing on top, the stabilization emitter array at the base. He pointed to the emitter array.

"Extraction nodes have a primary processing node on top and a data uplink array on the side. The processing node reads substrate frequency and converts it into usable data output. The data uplink sends that output to the monitoring network." He pointed to the top of the intact node. "This has a frequency regulation emitter. Not a processing node. It doesn't read and transmit — it maintains a consistent frequency output to stabilize the supply corridor's substrate state. The corridor runs through a high-variance substrate zone. Without stabilization, the variance causes interference in the transport vehicles' navigation systems."

Fen Corr was looking at the node. Looking at it with attention.

"The damaged one," he said.

Corin looked at the cracked housing. "The damaged one was also a stabilization node. The damage you caused disrupted its emitter output. The corridor's variance level will be elevated until the node is repaired." He paused. "The three supply runs scheduled for this corridor in the next forty-eight hours will have increased interference risk. The settlements along the route depend on the supply runs for medical equipment and food stores."

A pause.

"How long to repair?"

"By morning," Corin said. "The maintenance team is already notified. They'll be here at first light."

Fen Corr walked to the damaged node. He crouched at the base and looked at the emitter array housing, at the technical specifications plate mounted on the post. He looked at the cracked housing above him, at the dark indicator panel. He stood.

He walked to the nearest intact node and crouched at its base in the same way, examining the emitter array. The indicator lights cycled above him: green-green-amber, three seconds. He stood and looked at the top of the post — the frequency regulation emitter housing, not a processing node, not a data uplink. He was looking for the difference he had just been told about, which was the act of someone who needed to see it rather than be told it.

He stood for a moment.

"Ninety seconds," he said. "You said extraction nodes cycle at ninety seconds in active operation."

"In a high-yield zone, yes."

He looked at the intact nodes' indicator lights. Three-second cycles. He looked at this for a while.

"I've been looking at cycling indicators for two years," he said. "I didn't know the extraction rate changed the interval." He was not looking at Corin when he said it. He was looking at the lights. Then he looked at Corin. "Show me the difference" had been a genuine question. The answer had been a genuine answer. He stood with both of them now.

"Six of us," he said. "Six people for two years, holding operations that the framework is — absorbing."

"The framework has absorbed eight of your former colleagues. The movement is still in the field because six of you have kept it there."

"Because we don't trust that the rest is real."

"The acknowledgment motion is real. It's in the Council's record. The names are attached. Iris Vane's handwriting on the exhibit." Corin held the man's attention. "The eastern communities' engagement is real. Maret's documentation of the pre-calibration period is in the supplementary brief to the oversight committee. The Substrate Assessment Unit has a data discrepancy under investigation. The Exchange subsidiary relationship is being voided by motion." He paused. "I can't show you these things are real from here. I can tell you what's in the record."

"What's the record worth?"

"Right now? More than it was six months ago. Less than it needs to be." He held the honest version because the honest version was the only one that had any purchase. "The record is worth exactly what it produces. If it produces accountability, it's worth something. If it produces nothing, it was paper. We're in the middle of finding out."

Fen Corr looked at the node cluster one more time. The damaged node with its cracked housing, the two intact ones, the emitter arrays that were not extracting anything.

He looked at the security contingent.

"Are we being arrested?"

"The reconciliation framework's terms allow for a documented-incident-without-prosecution process if all parties agree." Corin said it clearly, because clarity was the only thing that worked here. "I need Dreya Cole's agreement. She was at the session tonight."

He called Dreya Cole. She picked up on the first ring.

"I know," she said. "I've been listening to your relay."

"Do you agree to the documented-incident process?"

A pause. A real pause, not a performative one — the pause of someone actually deciding.

"Yes," she said. "But they sit down with the families in the route settlements tomorrow. Same as the eight did before the session. Not as punishment. As a starting point."

He looked at Fen Corr.

"You heard that," Corin said. It was not a question.

Fen Corr looked at the two fighters behind him. They were looking back at him. Something passed between them that Corin could not fully read and did not try to.

"Yes," Fen Corr said. "We heard it."

The signal blockers came down. The security contingent moved forward. No one was arrested. Corin stood in the middle of the maintenance track in the dark with the northern night around him and the damaged node above him and the repair team's estimated arrival seven hours away.

He called Hollan: "Three fighters entering the reconciliation framework. Documented-incident process. Dreya Cole agrees."

"The other three?"

"Withdrew before I arrived. Their position is unknown."

"We'll track."

"The damaged node — the repair team needs to be here by first light. The supply runs are scheduled tomorrow."

"Already dispatched," Hollan said. "I called them when you called me."

Corin stood at the node cluster for a few more minutes after the security contingent and the three fighters had moved toward the settlement vehicles. The night was quiet. The unrepaired node's indicator lights were dark. The other two were cycling normally, their emitter arrays maintaining the supply corridor's substrate frequency in the way that stabilization nodes were supposed to — quietly, continuously, without processing or transmitting anything.

He looked at the intact nodes for a while. He thought about the fact that Fen Corr had asked to be shown the difference. Not told — shown. He had been told it for the duration of the conversation and had waited until the telling was done and then had said the one thing that the telling couldn't substitute for. Corin had walked him to the node. Fen Corr had looked at the emitter array. He had crouched and stood and walked to the damaged node and crouched and stood there too. The indicator lights on the intact nodes had been cycling through their three-second interval the whole time.

The conversation after that had taken four minutes. Fen Corr had asked two questions. The answers had been straightforward. Then he had looked at the security contingent and at Corin and had made his statement about six people for two years.

He wrote a note to Iris from the maintenance track before he got back in the vehicle: *Three fighters entering reconciliation framework. Documented-incident process, Dreya Cole's terms. Fen Corr asked to be shown the emitter array before he agreed to anything. He stood there for a while. I want that observation in the record — not interpreted, just noted: he needed to see it.*

He sent it. Iris would log it without the interpretation he had explicitly excluded, because the note had said *not interpreted, just noted.* She would enter the observation in the incident record's supplementary field exactly as he had written it. That was the correct handling. The observation was true — Fen Corr had stood at the emitter array — and the truth of it belonged in the record without a claim about what it meant.

Then he got in his vehicle and drove back toward the settlement house, where the session documentation was still on the table and Dreya Cole was still at the communication panel.

chapters.title: Vol-010, Chapter 7: The Standoff
chapters.text:
He had misread the alert.

The three fighters who had withdrawn before Corin arrived had not fully withdrawn — they had moved to the northern edge of the supply route's maintenance track, out of the Reckoner network's immediate monitoring range, and had been observing from cover during the entire conversation at the node cluster. Hollan's security team found them at two in the morning, which was forty minutes after the three at the cluster had agreed to the reconciliation framework process and thirty minutes after Corin had gotten back in his vehicle.

He turned around.

By the time he arrived at the northern position, the three fighters who had been in cover were standing in a loose semicircle with four of Hollan's security team positioned around them. No one was restrained. No one had weapons raised. It was the particular standoff that happens when all parties are waiting to find out what the situation is.

The nine minutes before Corin arrived — which Treveth described to him later, because Treveth had been documenting the interaction from the moment the security team found the three fighters — had been a standoff in the most literal sense: no one had moved. The security team had found the fighters and established a perimeter with a verbal statement of presence and intent, no escalation. The fighters had held position. There had been no communication for nine minutes. The security team had not advanced. The fighters had not retreated. They had been in the northern cover position for the duration of the cluster interaction, watching. When Treveth's team stated their presence, the fighters had made no move and said nothing for nine minutes. Then the youngest one had said: "We were watching." And then Corin had arrived.

The three fighters from the cover position were younger. Corin did not recognize any of them from the reconciliation framework documentation — they were not in the database of known Congregation operational personnel, which meant they were either recent additions to the six-fighter unit or had been operating under identification the tracking hadn't captured. The youngest one, a woman who looked to be in her early twenties, was watching the security team with the careful attention of someone who has been trained to watch for specific things and is finding the situation does not match the training.

The security team lead — a man named Treveth who had been on Hollan's northern zone team for three years — made a very small nod toward Corin when he arrived. Not a greeting. A handoff. You're here, you talk.

Corin walked into the semicircle space. He stood at the open point. He looked at the three fighters.

"You were watching," he said.

The youngest woman spoke. "We weren't leaving until we knew what happened to the others."

"They're in the reconciliation framework process. Documented incident, no prosecution. Dreya Cole agreed to the terms." He paused. "They're going to sit with the families along the supply route tomorrow. As a starting point."

The youngest woman looked at the other two. One of them — a man in his mid-thirties with a scar on his jaw — looked back at her without expression.

"The one you were talking to," the man with the scar said. "Fen. He left the Congregation before Joren's walk."

"I know."

"He came back because he thought the framework was a strategy, not a real process."

"What does he think now?"

The man with the scar was quiet. He was not evading — he was actually thinking, which Corin recognized because it looked different from evasion. It had a different quality of stillness.

"He explained the node," the man said finally. "Talked through the emitter array. I heard it from here."

"Were you surprised?"

"About the node?" He looked at the node cluster, visible from the northern position. The cracked housing of the damaged node. "Yes. We've been striking what we've been told are extraction points. The network tells you where the extraction pressure is coming from — the substrate variance, the areas where the frequency is off. We've been hitting nodes in high-variance areas."

"Because high variance feels like extraction."

"Doesn't it?"

"High variance in the substrate can be caused by extraction. It can also be caused by natural substrate instability in areas with complex underlying geology. And it can be caused by insufficient stabilization coverage — places where the stabilization infrastructure doesn't reach, or where it's been disrupted." He looked at the damaged node. "The northern supply corridor is a complex substrate zone. The nodes here are stabilization, not extraction. When you strike stabilization nodes in a high-variance area, you remove the stabilization and the variance increases. Which feels more like extraction."

The man with the scar was quiet for a long time.

"We've been making it worse," he said.

Not a question. A statement arrived at from evidence.

"In this location, yes. I can't speak to every location. Some of what the Congregation has targeted over the past two years has been extraction infrastructure. Some of it has been stabilization." He paused. "The reconciliation framework's documentation process includes infrastructure review — looking at what was struck and what it was. We've been building that assessment since the framework started. Some of the strikes were on correct targets. Some weren't."

"And the ones that weren't."

"We document them. We know the difference now. The communities that were affected know the difference — they've been living with it. The settlement councils in the northern zone have been in the framework sessions specifically because three of the disrupted supply runs from last year affected their medical equipment schedules."

The man with the scar was quiet for a long time. He was looking at the node cluster — visible from the northern position, the two intact nodes cycling their indicator lights, the dark housing of the damaged one. Three seconds. Green-green-amber. He had heard Fen Corr explain the emitter array from two hundred meters out in the dark. He had heard the explanation and had heard Fen Corr agree to the framework. He had stayed in cover for forty minutes after the security team found them, saying nothing, and now he was standing in the maintenance track at two in the morning thinking about the difference between extraction and stabilization.

"The framework's infrastructure review," he said. "The strikes assessed as stabilization strikes. How many."

"I don't have the exact count in my head. The assessment is ongoing — we add entries as new information becomes available. Some of the strikes are still under analysis."

"More than one."

"More than one."

He absorbed this.

"The communities that lost supply runs," he said. "Because of the stabilization disruption. They know it was us."

"They know strikes occurred. The framework's documentation doesn't name operationals publicly — the framework process is confidential for the ninety-day period after a documented incident. After that, the entries are in the general record but at the level of incident, not individual. The settlement councils know the Congregation struck the infrastructure. The framework is the process through which accountability happens."

"Accountability," the man said. Not contemptuous. Trying the word out, seeing if it fit the shape of what he was inside.

"The families Dreya Cole asked the first group to sit with," Corin said. "Those are the same families who were affected by the supply disruptions. The accountability is not abstract. It's those people."

He was quiet again. Then: "My name is Carr." It was not a request for anything. It was the beginning of identification, the first step of a person entering a record.

The youngest woman sat down. She sat down on the maintenance track's edge in the middle of a standoff at two in the morning, which was such a specific and unself-conscious act that Corin's attention went to it immediately. She sat down the way someone sits down when they are very tired and have been very tired for a long time and have reached the moment when the tiredness is more immediate than the standoff.

"How long has the framework been running?" she asked.

"Nine months."

"I've been operational for seven months," she said. "I joined the unit after the framework started." She was looking at the ground. "I was in the Teller territory when the framework sessions began. I heard about them. I asked the others if we were going to engage. They said not yet. We kept operating." A pause. "I kept operating. Because there were people who knew what the targets were and I trusted them."

"And now?"

"Now I'm sitting on a supply route in the middle of the night and a node that we thought was extracting turns out to be a stabilization emitter." She looked up. "I want to talk to the people in the framework. The eight who came in through Ash."

"They were at the session tonight."

"I know. I know they were. I want to talk to them not in a formal session. Before. The way Dreya Cole asked the others to sit with the families." She met his eyes. "Is that something that can happen?"

Corin looked at Treveth, who was listening. Treveth gave the smallest possible lift of his chin — not objection, not authorization, just: your call.

"I'll arrange it," Corin said. "It takes a few days to coordinate. The framework coordinator, Dreya Cole, the eight participants. But yes."

The youngest woman nodded. She got up from the ground. She stood up straight.

"We're entering the framework," she said. It was not a question either. It was the statement of something decided in the last five minutes. She looked at the other two. The man with the scar nodded once. The third fighter — who had not spoken at all — raised his chin in what was a nod.

"Three fighters," Corin said, for the record, and because Treveth was documenting the interaction. "Documented incident process. I need Dreya Cole's agreement."

He called Dreya Cole for the second time that morning. She had not gone to sleep. He could tell by the sharpness in her voice — not irritated, just awake, the sharpness of someone who had been waiting.

"Three more," he said.

"Same terms."

"She's asking to talk to the eight before the formal session. Informally. The way you asked the first three to sit with the families."

A pause. "She's right to ask. Yes. Tell her I'll arrange it."

He told her. The youngest woman listened. She did not say thank you, which he respected — the dynamic did not call for gratitude in that direction. She said: "I'm Sela," and that was the beginning of the documentation.

---

He did not drive back to the settlement house after this. He drove back to the node cluster, parked, and sat in the vehicle for twenty minutes while the night was quiet around him and the damaged node's dark indicator lights were visible through the windshield.

He looked at the node through the windshield and thought about the three groups. The first three at the cluster — Fen Corr and the two who had been with him. The three in the northern position — Carr, Sela, the third fighter who had raised his chin in a nod and had not yet said his name. Six people in two hours. He had not planned for two groups. The alert had described six fighters and had updated to say three had withdrawn. He had arrived expecting to talk to the three who remained and had ended the night talking to all six.

Six. Zero remaining in active operations.

He thought about Sela sitting down on the maintenance track edge. The specific quality of that — not strategic, not performed, the act of a person whose tiredness had simply become more immediate than the standoff. She had joined the unit seven months ago. She had been operational for seven months and had trusted the people who told her what the targets were, and now she was sitting on a supply route at two in the morning and the node she had thought was extracting had turned out to be a stabilization emitter. The tiredness was seven months of that discrepancy accumulating in the gap between what she had been told and what was actually in front of her.

He thought about what she had asked: to talk to the eight before the formal session. Not in the session — before it. The same structure Dreya Cole had required of the first group, except Sela had asked for it herself, without it being required. She wanted to understand the framework from the inside before she entered it officially. That was not the same as reluctance. That was something different, something closer to care.

He sat in the vehicle until the indicator lights from the two intact nodes were the clearest thing in his field of vision — green-green-amber, three seconds, the emitter arrays doing their quiet work.

Six fighters. Six people who had been the operational remainder of a movement that had lost its direction after Joren's walk, after the censure, after the framework started absorbing the people who wanted a way out. Six people who had kept going because stopping felt like losing something before there was anything concrete to show that stopping was worth it.

All six were now in the reconciliation framework.

Not in the formal session. Not with signed documentation yet. But in the framework, in the specific sense that they had agreed to the terms: the documented incident, the sitting with families, the conversation with the eight.

The movement was in the record now. All of it. The strikes and the stopping.

He thought about Fen Corr looking at the emitter array. *Show me the difference.* The genuine question, the man who had left the Congregation and come back because he thought the framework was strategy, standing in the dark and being shown the difference between extraction and stabilization. The thing that the Congregation had been striking because the substrate variance felt like extraction — the thing that had been making the variance worse — was a stabilization emitter.

He did not know if Fen Corr found this devastating or clarifying or both. He suspected both.

He sent Iris the incident report from the vehicle: *Two groups. Three at the node cluster, three in cover at the northern position. All six now in the reconciliation framework. Documented-incident process. Dreya Cole's terms. No arrests. Node repair team on the way. Full session arrangements in coordination.*

She replied at three forty-seven in the morning: *Logging it now. What category should I use for the incident record?*

He thought about this.

*Node strike,* he wrote. *Reconciliation framework outcome. Both.*

*Both,* she confirmed. *I'll document the full sequence.*

He drove back to the settlement house and slept for three hours in the spare room Dreya Cole had kept available. In the morning the repair team was at the node cluster when he passed it on the way out. The cracked housing was being replaced. By the time the first supply run scheduled for the corridor came through, the node would be running again.

He drove south. He had a committee session in three days.

chapters.title: Vol-010, Chapter 8: The Count
chapters.text:
The Friday committee session ran four hours. Lian presented the second field expedition analysis in the first hour: the signal intensification at the coordinate, the margin above the surrounding territory trend, Tael's account documented in the field notes. Committee Chair Rook asked four technical questions. Sira Olan asked three. Daves asked one, which was the most precise question of the seven: whether the signal pattern at the coordinate was consistent with the entity applying a deliberate, sustained process to a specific location over a measurable time period. Lian said: "The data is consistent with that characterization. I would not go beyond 'consistent with.'" Daves noted this. He did not ask her to go further.

In the second hour, the technical analyst presented the Substrate Assessment Unit data review. The forty-seven unrouted records. The classification's origin — the data management protocol, the second reorganization, the staff member who had applied it. The content analysis of the forty-seven records: post-calibration substrate readings from the eastern transitional zone, showing the substrate state in the area adjacent to the eastern communities for a period of seven years, beginning nine years after the calibration.

What the forty-seven records showed: the eastern transitional zone's substrate, in the years following the calibration, had exhibited a sustained elevated variance pattern that differed significantly from the pre-calibration baseline the monitoring archive held for that zone. The variance had diminished gradually over the seven years of active data collection but had not returned to the pre-calibration baseline by the end of the collection period. The records stopped when the commercial data-sharing contract ended.

"The variance in the transitional zone," Rook said. "Is that a direct effect of the calibration?"

"The technical analyst's analysis shows a correlation in timing — the variance elevation began approximately two years after the calibration and diminished over the following years. The calibration's specific effects on the eastern communities' substrate are documented in the monitoring archive for the communities themselves — the communities' substrate shows a different pattern than the transitional zone's, because the calibration acted directly on the communities' Resonance architecture rather than on the surrounding zone." The analyst paused. "The transitional zone variance is consistent with secondary substrate effects — the community's altered architecture producing variance in adjacent territory. This is a known mechanism in extreme Resonance architecture modification events."

"Secondary effects," Daves said. "The calibration altered the eastern communities' Resonance architecture, and that alteration produced substrate variance in the adjacent territory for at least seven years."

"The data shows variance consistent with that mechanism. Yes."

Rook looked at the forty-seven records' content summary for a long time. "The committee hasn't seen this data for nine years because of a data management protocol."

"Correct."

"The unit head wasn't aware the records had been classified out of the routing queue."

"The investigation's findings indicate she found the classification in year three of her tenure and noted it in her internal queries. She did not know the specific staff member responsible or the full context of the classification's application. Her queries about the classification received the same non-responses as her queries about the Exchange relationship."

Rook looked at Corin. "The unit head."

"Derel is not under investigation for wrongdoing," Corin said. "The investigation's scope was the data discrepancy. The discrepancy's origin is a nine-year-old data management classification applied by a staff member who was implementing a legitimate protocol in a way that produced an illegitimate outcome. The investigation findings are in the committee record."

"And the unit head."

"She placed seven years of documentation in my hands before the investigation began. That documentation is also in the committee record."

Rook nodded, very slowly. He moved to the third hour: the Exchange subsidiary structure motion's status. Cross briefed the committee for forty minutes. The motion was in deliberation. The built-in archive access order — which the legal staff had confirmed was procedurally feasible — was included in the motion's text. The Exchange's archive of eastern transitional zone data would be accessible for archive review simultaneously with the subsidiary structure's dissolution.

"When does the motion vote?" Sira Olan asked.

"Six days," Cross said.

"Do we expect it to pass?"

"I'm not in a position to count votes," Cross said. "Elara Venn would know."

Rook closed the formal session at one in the afternoon and asked Corin, Lian, Iris, and the analyst to stay. The others filed out.

"The forty-seven records," Rook said, when the room was empty. "The eastern communities. Have the people there been told?"

"Not yet," Corin said. "We were waiting for the content analysis to be complete before informing anyone in the communities. The analysis is complete as of this week."

"Tell them," Rook said. "Before the Exchange archive access happens. Before more findings come in from that process. The communities should have what we know before we find more."

"Iris has been planning that," Corin said.

Iris said: "I'll go through the documentation network first. The two hundred and sixteen contacts, then Maret for broader community distribution. The notification should be clear about what the data shows and what it doesn't show — it shows secondary substrate effects in the transitional zone, it does not show anything we didn't already know about the calibration's direct effects on the communities themselves."

"You know how to do this," Rook said.

"Yes."

He stood. The session was over. "Lian. The signal at the coordinate. Four months since the second expedition — is a third warranted?"

Lian looked at her notes. She looked at the pattern in the analysis. "The fixed monitoring arrays can detect the coordinate's signal now. It wasn't detectable on the fixed arrays before the second expedition — the signal has strengthened enough to register on the standard infrastructure. I can continue monitoring from the Citadel."

"Do that. Monthly reports."

"I'll add it to the monitoring schedule."

He left. The committee room emptied. Corin stayed for a moment, looking at the display that was still showing the forty-seven records' content summary — the seven-year post-calibration variance data, the elevated substrate state, the secondary effects of a structural removal conducted thirty-one years ago still visible in the data seven years later.

He turned off the display.

---

Iris received his incident report from the northern supply route two days after the session, which was when she had time to complete the documentation. She sat with it the way she sat with all documentation — she read it completely, noted the sequence, identified the categories.

The report was thorough — Corin had drafted it from his vehicle at three in the morning, sitting in the maintenance track with the damaged node's dark indicator panel visible through the windshield. It covered the alert receipt, the drive, the arrival at the node cluster, the conversation with Fen Corr, the reconciliation framework agreement, the documented-incident terms, the call to Dreya Cole, the second alert, the northern position, the nine-minute standoff as Treveth had described it, the conversation with the man with the scar, the youngest fighter's request to talk to the eight, Dreya Cole's terms, Sela's name, the three entering the framework. It ended with the node damage assessment and the maintenance team's expected arrival time, which had been confirmed by Hollan's dispatch at one forty-seven in the morning.

The documented-incident terms were specific. Corin had drafted them in coordination with Dreya Cole from the vehicle before he called in the second group: the incident — both groups' involvement in the node cluster strike and the cover position — was to be formally documented in the reconciliation framework's incident log with full source documentation and all parties' identification. The documentation would be sealed under the framework's confidentiality protocol for ninety days, after which it would enter the general framework record accessible to all parties. During the ninety-day period, the framework's legal team would review the incident documentation and confirm that the documented-incident process was complete. No prosecution for any offense arising from the node cluster incident would be pursued if the documented-incident process was completed correctly and all six participants fulfilled their engagement with the framework's terms: the sitting with families, in the case of the first three; the informal conversation with the eight, in the case of Sela's group; and for all six, attendance at the next formal framework session.

Node strike. Reconciliation framework outcome.

She opened the registry. She found the running count of Congregation incidents — the operations, the strikes, the documented events that had entered the framework's record over the past nine months. She added the new entry.

Below it, the count.

Six fighters reconciled. Zero in active operations.

She wrote the registry entry for the incident. The registry's incident section was a subsection she had created at the framework's founding session — not part of the core harm documentation, which was the two hundred and sixteen entries with their acknowledgment notations, but a parallel record of the Congregation's interaction with the restoration process over time. She had explained the category to Corin when she created it: the reconciliation framework's outcomes were part of the history of what the restoration had required, and the history of what the restoration had required was part of what the registry held. The incidents were not the same as the harm the calibration had caused, but they were part of the same story, and the registry was a complete story or it was not sufficient.

The entry for the northern supply route incident was four paragraphs: the incident date, the location, the node cluster identification, the damage assessment. The parties: six Congregation fighters, the security contingent, the Restoration Liaison. The terms: documented-incident process, Dreya Cole's agreement, the conditions. The outcome: all six in the reconciliation framework. At the bottom of the entry, cross-references: the three framework participant entries for the first group, the three pending entries for Sela and the others, the node damage repair confirmation from Hollan's team, the maintenance record from the repair crew.

She looked at the count for a moment. Then she looked at the full running incident log. The strikes that had been on extraction infrastructure. The strikes that had been on stabilization infrastructure. The documented community impact. The reconciliation framework outcomes — the sessions, the family meetings, the documented-incident processes.

She called Corin. It was the end of the day. She could tell by his voice that he was also finishing.

"Come to my office," she said. "Before you leave."

He came. She had the terminal open, the running count visible, the incident log beside it. She had also printed a single-page summary of the count — not the full log, just the current status: six fighters in the framework, zero in active operations, node damage repaired.

She put the summary on the desk between them.

"The movement is in the record now," she said. "All of it. The strikes and the stopping."

He looked at the summary. "Yes."

"The node they struck is repaired."

"By the morning after. Hollan's team."

She looked at the log. The long list of entries, starting nine months ago, each one dated and categorized and sourced. The operational history of a movement that had been trying to damage a thing and had not always known what the thing was. The reconciliation of each entry with the outcome: some of the targets had been wrong, some right, and the movement had stopped before the accounting was complete.

"The six people," she said. "The youngest one. Sela."

"She joined seven months ago. She was in the Teller territory when the framework started."

"She asked to talk to the eight before the formal session."

"Yes."

Iris was quiet. She had been doing this long enough to know the difference between the thing a person says when they are defeated and the thing a person says when they have decided to move. Sela had been asking what kind of process she was entering, not whether she was entering it.

"What comes next?" Corin asked.

She looked at the Substrate Assessment Unit data on the secondary terminal. The forty-seven records. The Exchange archive access order pending in the motion. Parren's contact with the unit — that thread was still unresolved, still sitting in Cross's metadata report, the timing and frequency of the correspondence logged but not interpreted.

"The unit has been processing the field monitoring data from the unrestored territories for seven years," she said. "If the entity's signal is strengthening in those territories — if the coordinate reading is detectable on the fixed arrays now — the unit is seeing that data. They've been seeing it since before we filed the expedition proposal."

He looked at her.

"Someone in that unit decided not to flag it," she said.

Not Derel — she had been asking questions for seven years. Not the staff member who had applied the classification nine years ago — that classification predated the fixed-array signal detection by years. Someone who, in the period since the signal became detectable on the fixed arrays, had been receiving the data and had not put it in the routing queue.

Three technical analysts in the unit. One of them was Haren, who had applied the original classification.

Corin looked at the terminal. He looked at the running count on the registry.

Zero in active operations. The movement in the record.

The unit's data, meanwhile, was still being processed. The signal was still strengthening. Someone in the Archive's own infrastructure was sitting on data about what the entity was doing, and the question of whether that was negligence or deliberate was not yet answered.

"I'll take it to Hollan tomorrow," he said.

"Tonight," she said. "It's the end of the day. He's still in his office."

He stood.

"Both categories need to exist," he said. He was not quoting her — he was arriving at the same place from a different direction. The movement in the record. The strikes and the stopping. The acknowledgment and the ongoing investigation. The restoration at 14.3 percent and the signal strengthening at the coordinate.

"Both categories need to exist," she said.

He went to find Hollan.

The corridor outside Iris's office was quiet at this hour. He walked through it the way he had been walking through Citadel corridors for two years — aware of the institutional tile, the fluorescent light, the ventilation hum that was the same in every corridor and had been the same since the Citadel was built. He had been inside this building for most of the past two years, working a problem whose resolution he could see in the trend data but not in the daily number. The daily number had not changed since the expedition returned. The trend was moving. Both were true at their own resolution. He held both as he walked.

Hollan's office was lit. Hollan was, as Iris had said, still there. Corin knocked and went in without waiting, because the thing Iris had identified — someone in the unit sitting on signal data about what the entity was doing at the coordinate — was not the kind of thing you waited on.
